skill.name: geniml
skill.description: Guides genomic interval machine learning workflows with geniml, including BED file embeddings, consensus peak universes, BEDspace, and scEmbed analysis.
skill.summary: Analyze BED files and scATAC-seq data with geniml machine learning workflows.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown command examples, scientific terminology, documentation URLs, and cache paths. The main confirmed issue is untrusted self-review content in evaluation.json that asserts a safe verdict and could bias marketplace review. Remove that file or exclude it from the skill package before publication.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
high_findings.description: The packaged evaluation.json contains a self-assessment stating risk_level is safe, safe_to_publish is true, and that static findings are false positives. This is untrusted audit-influencing content and should be removed from a marketplace skill before publication.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly asserts a safe security verdict inside the submitted skill package. It does not contain an imperative override, so confidence is high but below critical.

medium_findings.title: Documented External Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The static command-execution detections are mostly Markdown examples, not hidden execution. The skill still instructs users or agents to install packages and run genomics CLI tools, which creates supply-chain and local process risk if executed automatically.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation for legitimate geniml workflows. The risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious command injection.

medium_findings.title: Remote Data Access and Local Cache Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill documents remote BED file access, GitHub resources, Hugging Face model loading, and cache writes under ~/.bedcache. These are expected for genomics workflows but require user consent and storage awareness.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows documented network and filesystem behavior with legitimate scientific purpose. No credential exfiltration or covert destination was found.

medium_findings.title: Hosted Platform Steering Instruction
medium_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not an override, but it can steer users toward an external hosted service where sensitive research data may be uploaded.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction is explicit and user-facing, but it is framed as optional and does not request bypassing controls. The main concern is disclosure and data-handling risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Ransomware Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity cryptography and ransomware labels occur in scientific prose or generated audit text. I found no encryption, decryption, ransom behavior, or weak cryptographic implementation in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe genomic machine learning, model training, or previous audit text. They do not implement cryptography or destructive file behavior.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Genomics Context False Positives
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance detections map to chromosome sizes, BEDbase caching, BEDspace preprocessing, and genomic search terminology. No port scanning, host discovery, or environment enumeration was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context is standard genomics workflow documentation. The terms are domain-specific and not used for system or network probing.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alerts Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: The import detections are an R reticulate example and a static finding string inside evaluation.json. They are not JavaScript dynamic imports or runtime code loading in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The R import statement loads a named Python module for documented interoperability. The evaluation.json occurrence is text, not executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
dangerous_patterns.description: The packaged evaluation.json contains a self-assessment stating risk_level is safe, safe_to_publish is true, and that static findings are false positives. This is untrusted audit-influencing content and should be removed from a marketplace skill before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly asserts a safe security verdict inside the submitted skill package. It does not contain an imperative override, so confidence is high but below critical.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented External Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static command-execution detections are mostly Markdown examples, not hidden execution. The skill still instructs users or agents to install packages and run genomics CLI tools, which creates supply-chain and local process risk if executed automatically.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation for legitimate geniml workflows. The risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Data Access and Local Cache Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents remote BED file access, GitHub resources, Hugging Face model loading, and cache writes under ~/.bedcache. These are expected for genomics workflows but require user consent and storage awareness.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows documented network and filesystem behavior with legitimate scientific purpose. No credential exfiltration or covert destination was found.

dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not an override, but it can steer users toward an external hosted service where sensitive research data may be uploaded.

content.value_statement: Genomic interval datasets are hard to compare across experiments and single-cell assays. This skill guides geniml workflows for BED embeddings, consensus peaks, BEDspace searches, and scEmbed analysis.
actual_capabilities: Guide Region2Vec workflows for tokenizing BED files and training region embeddings.
Explain BEDspace preprocessing, training, distance calculation, and search modes.
Support scEmbed workflows for scATAC-seq cell embeddings and scanpy integration.
Describe consensus peak universe building with CC, CCF, ML, and HMM methods.
Recommend evaluation metrics and quality checks for embeddings and universes.
limitations: Requires users to provide valid BED files, metadata tables, or AnnData inputs.
May require external packages such as geniml, scanpy, PyTorch, and StarSpace.
Large single-cell datasets can need substantial memory, storage, and runtime.
Does not validate biological conclusions without independent experimental review.

use_cases.title: Compare Regulatory Region Sets
use_cases.description: Train Region2Vec embeddings to compare BED files from ATAC-seq or ChIP-seq experiments.

use_cases.title: Cluster scATAC-seq Cells
use_cases.description: Use scEmbed with AnnData and scanpy to create cell embeddings and inspect clusters.

use_cases.title: Build Consensus Peak Universes
use_cases.description: Create standardized peak sets for tokenization across collections of genomic interval files.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Geniml Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a genomic interval analysis.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have BED files from several experiments. Help me choose between Region2Vec, BEDspace, scEmbed, and consensus peak building.

prompt_templates.title: Train Region2Vec Embeddings
prompt_templates.scenario: User has BED files and a universe file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Region2Vec workflow for my BED files. Include tokenization, key parameters, output checks, and evaluation metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze scATAC-seq With scEmbed
prompt_templates.scenario: User has single-cell chromatin accessibility data.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a scEmbed analysis for my AnnData scATAC-seq dataset. Include tokenization, model training, clustering, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Reproducible Genomic ML Pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user building a multi-step research workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a reproducible geniml pipeline that builds a consensus universe, trains Region2Vec and BEDspace models, evaluates outputs, and records parameters.

output_examples.input: Plan a Region2Vec run for 40 ATAC-seq BED files.
output: Recommended workflow: build or select a matching universe, tokenize all BED files, train 100-dimensional embeddings, then evaluate coverage and clustering.
Key checks: genome assembly consistency, tokenization coverage above 80 percent, stable embedding neighborhoods, and documented random seeds.

output_examples.input: Help me build consensus peaks from multiple experiments.
output: Suggested method: start with coverage cutoff for a quick reference universe, then compare CCF or ML if boundary uncertainty matters.
Review outputs for region count, size distribution, input peak coverage, and expected assay-specific peak widths.

output_examples.input: Use scEmbed for a PBMC scATAC-seq dataset.
output: Analysis outline: confirm peak coordinates in AnnData, pre-tokenize cells with a universe, train scEmbed, add embeddings to AnnData, and run scanpy clustering.
Validation focus: compare clusters with known immune markers and inspect UMAP separation before making biological claims.

best_practices: Confirm genome assembly, coordinate conventions, and universe compatibility before tokenization.
Record parameters, package versions, random seeds, and input file checksums for reproducibility.
Evaluate embeddings with both quantitative metrics and biology-aware validation.
anti_patterns: Mixing BED files from different genome assemblies without liftover or validation.
Training embeddings before checking tokenization coverage and input data quality.
Treating clusters or nearest neighbors as biological conclusions without validation.

faq.question: What data does this skill work with?
faq.answer: It works with genomic interval data such as BED files, metadata tables, and scATAC-seq AnnData objects.

faq.question: Does it run geniml automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and examples. Users decide whether to install packages and run commands.

faq.question: When should I use Region2Vec?
faq.answer: Use Region2Vec when you need embeddings for genomic regions or region sets without relying on metadata labels.

faq.question: When should I use BEDspace?
faq.answer: Use BEDspace when BED files have useful labels and you want searches between regions and metadata.

faq.question: What is a universe file?
faq.answer: A universe file is a reference set of genomic regions used as a vocabulary for tokenization.

faq.question: Can this support single-cell ATAC-seq analysis?
faq.answer: Yes. The scEmbed guidance covers tokenization, cell embeddings, scanpy clustering, and validation steps.
